topic: The Nazca Lines, Nazca Desert, Peru
hook_question: THEY'RE ONLY WHOLE\NFROM THE SKY.\NBUILT 1,500 YEARS\NBEFORE FLIGHT.
iconic_detail: Hundreds of giant geoglyphs etched into the Peruvian desert by the Nazca culture between roughly 500 BCE and 500 CE — a hummingbird, a monkey, a spider, a condor, some over 1,200 feet across — are so large that on the ground they look like nothing but scuffed dirt. They only resolve into recognizable shapes from hundreds of feet up, and no one who carved them could have ever seen that view. They weren't rediscovered until commercial pilots started flying over the desert in 1927.
_voice_status: PENDING — reconfirm ElevenLabs Creator quota via check_subscription before render; fall back to SILENT (captions-only, silent AAC track) if 0 remaining, matching w28-beaumont precedent.

chapters.voiceover:
In the Nazca Desert of southern Peru, someone carved hundreds of drawings into the ground. A hummingbird. A monkey. A spider. Some over a thousand feet long.

From where you're standing next to them, they look like nothing. Just scraped lines in the dirt.

But fly a few hundred feet up, and they snap into perfect shapes.

The Nazca people who made these, sometime between 500 BCE and 500 CE, had no aircraft. No way to ever see their own work whole.

They weren't rediscovered until pilots started flying over this desert in 1927. Fourteen centuries after the last line was cut.
